text: The video shows a left hydrosalpinx and a left chocolate cyst with severely adherent intestines on the left side, posterior to the uterus and pelvic wall. The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland forceps to desiccate and scissors to cut, carefully separating intestinal adhesions from the left pelvic wall to avoid injury to the left ureter during the salpingectomy.

Previously, I think somebody has done laparoscopy, and they felt that we cannot do salpingectomy or a tubal clipping. And then patient, unfortunately, went back to the highway people, and then again highway people felt that let it send to a higher center where they can do intestinal racializes. So we have seen her video previously before doing this surgery, and we were anticipating that there may be a, severe addictions and accordingly we have done bowel preparation, counselling if required. We have not required GI surgeon in this case but if required we may require GI surgeon also. This is what the preliminary consent must be taken from the patient.

The surgeon carefully dissects the adherent intestine from the posterior uterine wall and pelvic wall using a closed Maryland forceps, which is then opened to create space. The bipolar forceps are positioned on the left side to avoid injury to the intestine. Suction irrigation is used to clean the area, revealing a tubular structure near the lateral uterine wall as the dissection progresses towards the hydrosalpinx.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the left hydrosalpinx and chocolate cyst, using suction irrigation to manage potential leakage of chocolate fluid. The left hydrosalpinx tube is visible, and the rectum is identified in the midline. The procedure aims to remove the left-sided masses to prevent disturbance of embryo implantation.

The surgeon performs dissection on the retroperitoneal tissue behind the left hydrosalpinx, ensuring the ureter and internal iliac vessels are identified and preserved. Both tubular structures, including the hydrosalpinx, are visible. The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and scissors to perform salpingectomy, removing the tissue through the umbilical port using a 5mm scope, with morselation as needed.

The surgeon performs copious suctioning and places a rectal probe to ensure the rectum is not injured. The ureter and pelvic vessels are clearly visible. The right hydrosalpinx has been previously removed, and the left side shows severe intestinal adhesions. The uterus is visible in the midline, with the pelvic floor on the right side. A drain is placed where the hydrosalpinx was removed to manage any potential fluid collection, although there is no visible oozing.
